sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 3-month history of cough, which initially started 4 months ago while loading horses and increased after a cold.
The patient reports that the cough has gone away when they exercise and bike.
The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
The patient stopped smoking 7 years ago.
The patient's mother had breast cancer but is currently doing well.
The patient has no previous history of lung infections or pneumonia.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis and was a previous smoker.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications: Methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and the patient is afebrile.

Physical exam: The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilateral when auscultated, the heart has a regular rate and rhythm with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs, and the extremities have intact pulses and brisk capillary refill.
The patient has a little bit of swelling to the knuckles on each hand, which is likely due to rheumatoid arthritis.
Test Results: The patient had a chest CT that showed a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which is smooth in appearance and has no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule that requires further evaluation and management.
Problem list: Lung nodule confirmed; Rheumatoid arthritis; History of smoking; Family history of breast cancer.
Plan:
I will order a pulmonary function test to establish a baseline for the patient's lung function.
I will also schedule a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
The patient will undergo a video-assisted thoracoscopy to remove the nodule and a small portion of the lung.
The patient will be under general anesthesia and will require a chest tube until the following day.
The patient will be admitted to the hospital for one night and discharged the next day.
The patient should continue to follow up with their rheumatologist and continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
Follow-up: The patient will be discharged the day after the procedure.
